slides.title: The Big Spaghetti 🍝
slides.content:
Right now, we write all our code straight down inside the `main` method.

Imagine writing a complex game entirely inside `main`. It would be 10,000 lines of messy, unreadable 'spaghetti code'. 

We need a way to organize code into named, reusable chunks.

slides.title: Enter the Method 🔧
slides.content:
A **Method** (sometimes called a Function in other languages) is a block of code with a designated name. 

Instead of writing 20 lines of code to spawn a boss, you put those 20 lines inside a method called `spawnBoss()`. Then, whenever you want to spawn the boss, you just call that name!

quizData.question: What is the primary purpose of writing your own Methods?

options.text: To organize and reuse code without copying and pasting.
To make the program run faster.

quizData.explanation: Methods wrap up code into labeled, reusable blocks. It keeps your code organized and strictly follows the DRY principle!

slides.title: Writing a Method ✍️
slides.content:
Methods live *inside* the `class`, but *outside* of `main`.

Look at how we define `greetPlayer()` below. (Ignore the words `static void` for now, we'll explain them later).

quizData.question: What is required to make the code *inside* the `greetPlayer()` method actually run?

options.text: Nothing, it runs automatically on startup.
You must explicitly 'call' it by name inside the main block.

quizData.explanation: Java only automatically runs what is inside `main()`. If you write a custom method, Java ignores it until you directly call it by name from inside `main` (or another active method).
